turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: [NS] We'll hear argument next in number oh oh nineteen thirty-seven, Jo Anne Barnhart versus Cleveland Walton. [NS] Mr. Lamken.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court. Congress descri- des- Congress designed the Social Security disability programs to provide benefits to workers who suffered from long term disabilities, individuals who were, in effect, forced into premature reti- retirement by a severe impairment. Consistent with the program's origin...

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Just a moment, Mr. Lamken. Spectators are admonished. Do not talk until you get out of the courtroom. The Court remains in session. Go ahead.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: Consistent with the program's origin, purpose, and text, the Commissioner has, for forty-five years, from the program's incetion [: inception] in adjudicating tens of millions of claims, and throughout repeated amendments, adhered to a single and consistent, reasonable construction of the definition of disability. To be disabled, a claimant must have an impairment that has lasted or can be expected to last at twelve m- least twelve months at a disabling level of severity, that is, the impairment must preclude substantial gainful activity for the twelve months during which it must last.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Well, now, presumably though, as I understand it, the Commissioner could evaluate a claim and start benefits in less than twelve months. [Inaudible]

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: Correct, Your Honor. That's preci- that's because the statute allows the Commissioner to award benefits if the im- if the disabling impairment can be expected to last twelve months. So, a- for...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: can disregard possibly periods of work during that period if the commission is satisfied that this is going to be a permanent impairment.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: Yes, if the work does not evidence an ability to engage in substantial gainful activity.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: That seems sort of inconsistent with your view on the second question that trial work periods shouldn't begin until the end of the twelve months.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: What What provision in the language of the statute says that you can't start a trial work period until the end of twelve months?

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: Again, it's not our position that you can't start a work period a trial work period nintel [: until] and the end of t-

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: until the end of twelve months.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: Right, unless they are entitled to benefits.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: And it says, a p- tr- a period of trial work for any individual shall begin with the month in which he becomes entitled to disability insurance benefits.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: the the difference is the other side says that he becomes entitled not when he is adjudicated to be entitled, but he becomes entitled when, in fact, all of the conditions exist for which the be- the benefits are payable.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: Because the statute provies [: provides] that the individual is entitled to benefits if he has a disability which can be expected to last twelve months or has lasted twelve months.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: trial work statute itself says that any services rendered by an individual during a period of trial work shall be deemed not to have been rendered in determining whether his disability has ceased.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: Right, in determining whether his disability has ceased.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: But I thought you took the position in y- I t- I thought you did not take the position in your brief that as a matter of law there had to be an adjudication.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: thought you said that ordinarily that's the way it it it works in practice, but that the real point you were making was that if the individual went back to work prior to the adjudication and therefore showed that in fact the disability or or the incapacity and hence the the the disability, as you see it, is is not going to last, that that that's the end of the matter.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: I I think you've precisely described our position, Justice Souter.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: you would not allow him to come in and say long before I came in for an adjudication, I was in fact disabled and entitled to benefits.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: on that on that can be expected I m- m-

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: actually the contrary view would make creates a rather...

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: of adjudication to make and one that's entirely foreign to the law.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: You got to look back six months and figure out whether six months ago he he he could be expected to...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: So, the real real significance of the...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: present tense is that it kind of gives an opportunity for the application of a kind of best evidence rule.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: do we give here? At the time this claim was evaluated, the regulations hadn't been adopted.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: that the Commissioner is entitled to Chevron deference like regardless of when the claim was adjudicated.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: The The f- The court of appeals here said that that simply wasn't consistent with the statute and it struck me that they had a very good argument just looking at the statute that that you are kind of ar- r- reduced to some fairly odd statutory construction to justify your position.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: we just say that we looked to the statute itself for what Congress' intent was?

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: find the statute in in the...

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: It's four twenty-three D two A and D one A which appear on pages sixty-nine just the heading of D one appears on sixty-nine A and seventy A of the appendix to the petition for a writ of certiorari.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: Turning at page seventy, th- it p- establishes two requirements that are interrelated.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: Justice Scalia, that might be one way of reading the statute,

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I don't see how else you can read it.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: See, my understanding of your position is y- you're saying that impairment, given the context of the statute, means a constantly, continuously disabling impairment, and disabling means that you are not equipped to engage in substantial what- whatever SGA stands substantial gainful activity.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: I- In effect, we're arguing, yes, that the type of impairment that Congress was referring to is an impairment that prevents substantial gainful activity because that's the type of impairment that is referenced in the statute.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: And if it isn't if it doesn't prevent substantial gainful activity, then it isn't an impairment within the meaning of the section.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: It is not the type of impairment that you must have for twelve months.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: just at the word impairment and what it means, you get back to the a- inability to do substantial gainful employment for that interval of time.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: Your Honor, one way you could lo- you could put your p- emphasis on the word impairment.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: I don't know why you don't focus on the word which.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: I think as a grammatical matter, Justice Stevens, we have to confess that the word which refers to the impairment because one would not inordinarily [: ordinarily] expect the substantial gainful activity to result in death or the inability better still, the inability to engage in substantial gainful activity to result in death.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: expect the inability to be expected to last to result in death where it lasted for a continuous period of twelve months? I don't know why.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: Well, Justice Stevens, we'd be perfectly willing to accept that if that were your view, but for purpose of this case, we we have for grammatical purposes, we believe that the phrase, which can be expected to result in death, modifies the impairment, not the inability to engage in substantial gainful act-

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: So, you you don't think it means both of which can be expected, just means one of which can be expected.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Where Where is the definition of impairment? I'm...

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: It's on s- page seventy-one A, Justice Scalia, after the numeral three of the joint of of the appendix to the petition for writ of certiorari.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: I'm sure that's necessarily a very precise reading of that section.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: The impairment is what results from the abnormalities.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: So, if i- the condition is, in this case schizophrenia, the impairment would be the inability to focus, the inability to p- think clearly, the impairment of the cognitive functions is another way of putting it.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: th- actually not because you can have these, u- not the trial work, but the unsuccessful work episodes.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: I- th- I That that is part of the definition of substantial gainful activity.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: So, the term substantial modifies er is directed to the length of the activity, not the daily intensity of the activity.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: It probabl- It It the substantiality does modify the intensity of the activity, but when terms of determining what kind of evidence shows that you're able to engage in that activity, the Commissioner has legislative authority to determine whether work excuse me, whether certain types of work attempts evidence your ability to engage in substantial gainful activity.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: this case is unusual on the facts because he came very close to making the one year limit, and there is the anomaly that, say, the person is approved for receiving disability benefits in month eight and then will be able to go on for two months past the time that his disability ceases.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: In fact, Your Honor, there were two adjudications in this case in a very short period of time within the filing of benefits.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Mr. Lamken, you said one of the th- things you said is i- if the Fourth Circuit view prevailed, th- there would be a large increase in the number of people who qualified for disability benefits.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Is there any report of any swell in the approved applications in the Fourth Circuit?

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: Your Honor, I have do not have any information regarding that.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: If we want to say, and I take it you want us to say, that if the agency has followed a consistent and well settled i- interpretation policy over a long course of time, that's entitled to special deference, is that just Chevron, or is there some other case that tells us this?

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: What's the best case authority is what I mean...

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: Your Honor, we believe this is a a classic case of Chevron deference.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: that talks about the length of time that the administration has followed...

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: Oh, well, that would be classically Me- if you're what you're referring to i- is Mead or Skidmore deference, yes.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: No, it's Skidmore, Chevron, and an- n- nothing...

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: I- I- Those are the two f- standards of deference with which I'm familiar, and in fact we believe that we'd win under either one.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: that falls in, technically, a deference category, but it gets you to the same point.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: Well, it may it certainly adds weight to the re- agency's view and it may make it so that there is only one reasonable construction of a statute this ambiguous.

turns.name: KATHRYN L. PRYOR
turns.text: micher [: Mr.] Chief Justice, and may it please the Court This case is governed by the plain language of the statutes, the language of the disability definition, which is the primary focus of the first issue presented, and the language of the trial work period and entitlement statutes, which are the primary focus of the second issue presented.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Cus [: Miss] Pryor, what what time limit do you pick? I mean, suppose a person has a disability that i- indeed w- is expected to last more than twelve months but it only has caused him to black out and be incapable of working for one minute.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Your Honor, I believe that that...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: one minute blackout entitle him to come under the program?

turns.name: KATHRYN L. PRYOR
turns.text: shows that a person must be under a disability during the five month waiting period.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: then they would then be entitled to benefits.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: that? What establishes the five months as as...

turns.name: KATHRYN L. PRYOR
turns.text: entitlement statute, Your Honor, which is set out at four twenty-three A...

turns.name: KATHRYN L. PRYOR
turns.text: which is found at on page sixty-four A and sixty-five A.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: reading from on sixty-four A m-

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: What part of the page? What part of page sixty-

turns.name: KATHRYN L. PRYOR
turns.text: Shall be entitled to a disability insurance benefit for each month beginning with the first month after his waiting period.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: So, that would be the sixth month every time.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: And i- i- if you started in this in month six let's take this case.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: So, star- it starts six months after he has been disabled and it continues until when?

turns.name: KATHRYN L. PRYOR
turns.text: Your Honor, he because the trial work period statute dictates that a person is entitled to a trial work period beginning in the first month he's entitled to benefits, he would be entitled to benefits beginning in April nineteen ninety-five, which is when he met all criteria of entitlement, and he would be entitled to a trial work period beginning that month.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: case, when he s- if he sta- he starts at month six, how many months actually would he be getting disability benefits?

turns.name: KATHRYN L. PRYOR
turns.text: He returned to substantial ga- well, he returned to any work in May of nineteen ninety-five, and he would be entitled to a nine month trial work period plus the additional three months, and then for any months during that thirty-six month entitlement reentitlement...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: period during which he could not work.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: if you could just give me the the time frame.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: end the minimum end would be when?

turns.name: KATHRYN L. PRYOR
turns.text: It would be his his actual work started end of May June, then nine months, plus additional three months, so approximately fourteen months I believe.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: So, some contider- -siderable time after he has been restored to substantial gainful activity.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: period is over, making substantial gainful activity, even though, as you say, his impairment continued, he would not be disabled.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: So So the basic statutory problem I think you I I see where you're you're debating whether you become entitled after five months or twelve months, and that seems to turn on the language in seventy A.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: problem is, to be absolutely frank, I think you could b- read it at that level severity or you don't have to read it at that level of severity.

turns.name: KATHRYN L. PRYOR
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, my response is that that requires this Court to read language into the into the...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: refers to an animal, you certainly can read in that it doesn't mean fleas.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: so you have to make some assumption.

turns.name: KATHRYN L. PRYOR
turns.text: Your Honor, I believe that the reading the plain language of the statute and the Commissioner has conceded this point that th- the phrase, which can be expected to result in death and which has lasted or can be expected to last for at least twelve months, refers to the impairment, not to the inability to g- engage in substantial gainful activity.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Ms. Pryor, I think you heard ma- Ms. Pryor?

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: The What I asked Mr. Lamken are you saying, in effect, that the word impairment means a disabling impairment.

turns.name: KATHRYN L. PRYOR
turns.text: Your Honor, I believe the entitlement provisions answer that question.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: The ca- case I gave you it's it's a a condition that can be disabling.

turns.name: KATHRYN L. PRYOR
turns.text: I believe that the entitlement statute requires that it be disabling for at least five months, the whit- the waiting period.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: for the twelve months, it doesn't matter what the level of severity is, but for the five months, you must be unable to engage in substantial gainful m-

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: What about about F F three? Let me look for it What am I talking about? I'm talking about section four twenty-three F three which says that a...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I'm I'm reading from my own copy of the...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: It says that a person who has been getting benefits may be determined not to be entitled to benefits on the basis of a finding that the physical or mental impairment, on the basis of which such benefits are provided, has ceased, does not exist, or is not disabling only if such finding is supported by.

turns.name: KATHRYN L. PRYOR
turns.text: I believe what this was referring to Your Honor, is that the medical improvement must or in this case the new or or improved diagnostic techniques, must result in that person's then their condition no longer being of such severity as to prevent substantial gainful activity.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: all it requires, and that therefore the individual is able to engage in substantial gainful activity.

turns.name: KATHRYN L. PRYOR
turns.text: Your Your Honor, I believe that this whole section refers to termination of disability...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: benefits for somebody who's already d- okay...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: but what would apply to termination gives you some indication of of what what the initial condition is is expected to be.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: continuous inability to to to work.

turns.name: KATHRYN L. PRYOR
turns.text: Th- There is a severity requirement which is part of the the definition, yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: And I I would not disagree with that.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: that severity requirement has nothing to do with duration, and this termination provision suggests that it does have something to do with...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: you can engage in any substantial gainful activity, you're terminated, assuming that there's been the improvement in the condition.

turns.name: KATHRYN L. PRYOR
turns.text: Your Honor, once there is improvement and and you are now able to engage in substantial gainful activity, I would agr- -gree that you could be terminated.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: one week after the five month period the person goes back to work? Would that mean they just got benefits for a week?

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: improvement b- be the ability to go back to work which they could not do before.

turns.name: KATHRYN L. PRYOR
turns.text: Their ability to go back to the fact that they had gone back to work would then be c- protected by a trial work period, and tha- and would be that the fact that they were working would not be able to be considered as evidence that they were no longer disabled.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: May I also ask you? I know you think it not legally significant, but do you disagree with the Government's position that they have consistently interpreted the statute in this way for forty years?

turns.name: KATHRYN L. PRYOR
turns.text: I would agree that they have consistently as- interpreted the statute...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: And would you agree that they correctly describe the legislative deliberations in nineteen fifty-six I think it was? But you just say they're totally irrelevant.

turns.name: KATHRYN L. PRYOR
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, I would I have referred to the legislative history in nineteen fifty-four, which is when that definition of disability was first in- defined, and in that instance, the nineteen fifty-four Senate report delineated two aspects of disability evaluation, a medically determinable impairment of serious proportions which is expected to be of long continued and indefinite duration and a present inability to engage in substantial gainful activity by reason of that impairment.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: One other thing I just wonder about in a case like this.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: but but I mean the twelve months versus five months as the...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: And on question two, if I understand you correctly, there is substantial agreement in the courts of appeals that you're correct?

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: that's not the case with question one.

turns.name: KATHRYN L. PRYOR
turns.text: Question one has been ruled on differently by different courts.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: two hinges on question if you lose on question one, you lose on question two, don't you?

turns.name: KATHRYN L. PRYOR
turns.text: No, Your Honor, I don't believe we do.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: D- D- Do you agree with the Government's estimate that m- that if if you win in this case, the difference in in payouts under this program is going to be something like eight billion dollars a year? Is it is it that excessive?

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Can I ask you before y- can I just ask you quickly if you're s- simply repeating these, which I is that o- on question two, am I right in thinking we don't reach question two if you lose on question one?

turns.name: KATHRYN L. PRYOR
turns.text: No, Your Honor, I don't believe that is...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: but what I didn't understand is if you lose on question one and your client therefore is not entitled to Social Security,

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: the end of the case? We don't have to reach question two.

turns.name: KATHRYN L. PRYOR
turns.text: definition of disability isn't the final part of that because a person can if the entitlement statute requires a prospective consideration of the criteria of entitlement, a p- every person who's insured, has not attained retirement age, has filed an application, is under a disability shall be entitled to disability insurance benefits for each month beginning with the first month after his waiting period.

turns.name: KATHRYN L. PRYOR
turns.text: he had a disability even if you look at...

turns.name: KATHRYN L. PRYOR
turns.text: If he even if there was at that time an expectation that his inability to engage in substantial gainful activity must last twelve...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: th- th- tha- th- Oh th- I'm going have to you're going to have to write say somebody's going to have to write this.

turns.name: KATHRYN L. PRYOR
turns.text: he is not entitled to a...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: to decide, for whatever theories you may have, whether he is or is not entitled.

turns.name: KATHRYN L. PRYOR
turns.text: difference with what you're saying, Your Honor, is that he can be entitled even if you find the duration applies to the inability to engage in...

turns.name: KATHRYN L. PRYOR
turns.text: And that is because the entitlement statute is a prospective standard from the point of application, and the...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: the position that it doesn't have to be decided, that the statute itself says...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: person is entitled at the end of the five months.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: But if but if if we determine that in fact the disability level must be as the Government claims, then in a case in which the determination is made before the twelve month period and at that time the person is back working at a substantial level so that the determination is properly made that the person never qualified, then there would be no r- reason, in a case like that, ever to proceed to the second question.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: May I ask this one question? I just want to be sure I haven't missed something in the argument.

turns.name: KATHRYN L. PRYOR
turns.text: Your Honor, that is set out in D two of four twenty-three, which is it's found at page seventy A.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: you, you're saying that the impair- that the severity requirement is an inability to engage in substantial work.

turns.name: KATHRYN L. PRYOR
turns.text: Well, but yes, b- in considering you can't do your past work or other work considering age, education, and work experience.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: It seems to me that I I I really have trouble understanding that if there's a seber- severity requirement measured by inability to work for a period of time, why that severity requirement wouldn't continue for the entire period under the statutory definition of impairment.

turns.name: KATHRYN L. PRYOR
turns.text: Well, first of all, Your Honor, the severity requirement does not have a durati- durational element to it.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: say so, but the but the but the disability has a durational requirement and it's not a disability unless it's severe enough.

turns.name: KATHRYN L. PRYOR
turns.text: And And there is a separate severity requirement, but I don't believe that the statute says that that it must last at that level of severity for twelve months.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: tell us what's happened to the respondent in the interim?

turns.name: KATHRYN L. PRYOR
turns.text: Your Honor, he subse- this is not in the record, but he subsequently reapplied and is on disability benefits based on the subsequent application.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: I would like to begin by addressing the five month trial work excuse me, the five month waiting period.

turns.name: THE MARSHAL
turns.text: The honorable court is now adjourned until Tuesday, the twenty-second of January, at ten o'clock.
